The findings will inform the mechanistic understanding of epigenetic memory in cancer and the potential long-term side effects of epigenetic therapies.
proposals.novelty: While most studies focus on removing methylation to promote differentiation, this proposal systematically investigates the inverse: whether adding methylation can actively block it. It uniquely integrates targeted epigenetic editing with high-resolution longitudinal profiling to dissect causality, building directly on Dr. Jones's findings regarding the persistence of pharmacologically induced hypermethylation.

proposals.title: Dissecting Independent Epigenetic Driver Systems in Cancer: A High-Throughput, Multi-Omic Analysis of DNA Methylation, Histone Modifications, and RNA Silencing
proposals.motivation: The prevailing model in cancer epigenetics posits that DNA methylation, histone modifications, and RNA silencing form a stable, interdependent regulatory network. However, this assumption of obligate crosstalk has not been rigorously tested. Recent work, including from our lab on chromosome-specific retention of hypermethylation after DNMT1 inhibition, suggests these systems may have independent dynamics. Determining if one system can act as a primary, independent driver of oncogenic silencing is crucial for understanding therapeutic resistance and designing targeted treatments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in specific cancer subtypes or genomic contexts, one epigenetic silencing system (DNA methylation, H3K9me3, or RNAi) can initiate and maintain pathological gene repression independently of the canonical reinforcement from the other two systems.
proposals.method: We will use patient-derived cancer cell lines and primary tumor samples (TCGA/other pre-2026 repositories). We will perform integrated ChIP-seq (H3K9me3, H3K4me3), whole-genome bisulfite sequencing, and small RNA-seq. Data will be analyzed using a bespoke computational pipeline to identify genomic loci (focusing on imprinted regions and transposable elements) where one repressive mark is present in the absence of the other two. We will functionally validate independent drivers using CRISPR/dCas9 systems to selectively erase single marks (e.g., targeted DNA demethylation with dCas9-TET1) and monitor gene expression and chromatin state.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a high-resolution map of independent vs. cooperative epigenetic silencing in cancer. It will identify specific disease contexts where therapies targeting a single system (e.g., DNMT inhibitors) may be uniquely effective or prone to failure. The findings will refine models of epigenetic dysregulation and inform more precise combination therapies.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the foundational assumption of stable three-system interaction, moving beyond correlation to test causal independence. It leverages Dr. Jones's expertise in methylation dynamics and imprinting to dissect this question in a disease-relevant, high-throughput framework not previously applied to this specific hypothesis.

proposals.title: Genome-wide mapping of DNA methylation signatures predictive of glucocorticoid sensitivity in acute lymphoblastic leukemia
proposals.motivation: While steroid resistance is a major cause of treatment failure in cancers like acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL), the epigenetic drivers of steroid sensitivity remain poorly defined. Identifying these drivers could reveal new biomarkers for patient stratification and novel targets for epigenetic priming to enhance therapeutic response, a critical need in pre-2026 oncology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, genome-wide DNA methylation patterns, particularly at enhancer regions and transposable elements, are associated with and functionally contribute to enhanced glucocorticoid sensitivity in ALL cells.
proposals.method: We will perform high-throughput reduced-representation bisulfite sequencing (RRBS) on a panel of well-characterized ALL cell lines stratified by glucocorticoid sensitivity. Differential methylation analysis will be conducted using the *DSS* algorithm. Candidate loci will be functionally validated using CRISPR-dCas9-TET1/3a for targeted demethylation and dCas9-DNMT3a for targeted methylation in isogenic backgrounds, followed by steroid response assays. We will integrate methylation data with existing histone mark and chromatin accessibility profiles to identify regulatory elements.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel epigenetic signature predictive of steroid sensitivity, moving beyond the study of resistance. It will establish a causal link between specific methylation marks and drug response, providing a framework for epigenetic patient stratification and the development of DNMT inhibitor-based 'priming' strategies to improve outcomes.
proposals.novelty: This study flips the dominant paradigm from investigating resistance to defining the active epigenetic state of sensitivity. It uniquely focuses on the functional role of methylation at non-promoter regulatory elements and transposable sequences, which are known to be dysregulated in cancer but not systematically studied in the context of steroid responsiveness.

proposals.title: Targeted DNMT1 Inhibition Induces Transposable Element Mobilization and Alters Local Mutation Spectra in Colorectal Cancer Models
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including from Dr. Jones's lab, shows that pharmacological inhibition of DNMT1 causes locus-specific DNA hypomethylation in cancer cells. The canonical view is that such epigenetic dysregulation primarily affects gene expression. However, hypomethylation of repetitive elements, particularly transposable elements (TEs), is a known feature of cancer genomes and is hypothesized to contribute to genomic instability, but a direct, causal link between targeted epigenetic therapy, TE mobilization, and de novo mutation accumulation has not been experimentally established in patient-derived models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sustained, targeted inhibition of DNMT1 in colorectal cancer cells leads to the demethylation and transcriptional activation of specific families of transposable elements, resulting in their mobilization and directly increasing the local mutation rate through insertional mutagenesis and associated DNA repair processes.
proposals.method: We will use patient-derived colorectal cancer organoids and isogenic cell lines treated with a DNMT1-specific inhibitor (as per Jones's prior work). We will perform whole-genome bisulfite sequencing and RNA-seq to identify hypomethylated and transcriptionally active TE loci. To directly capture new TE insertions, we will employ a combination of targeted L1-seq and a novel computational pipeline (TE-MAP) that integrates split-read and discordant read-pair analysis from high-coverage, longitudinal whole-genome sequencing of clonally expanded treated cells. Mutation spectra will be analyzed using MutSig and custom scripts to correlate with hypomethylated TE domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence that pharmacological targeting of an epigenetic regulator can be a proximate cause of increased mutation rates via TE mobilization. It will redefine the mechanistic understanding of epigenetic therapies, highlighting a potential risk factor (genomic instability) alongside therapeutic benefit. The findings will also establish a novel experimental and computational framework for assessing the mutagenic impact of epigenetic dysregulation.
proposals.novelty: While TEs are known to be hypomethylated in cancer, demonstrating their active mobilization and mutagenic consequence as a direct result of targeted DNMT1 inhibition—rather than as a passive correlate of global hypomethylation—is a novel and non-obvious causal mechanism. This bridges the fields of epigenetic therapy and genome stability in a patient-relevant model system.

proposals.title: A Pan-Cancer Atlas of DNA Methylation Dynamics: Integrating Whole-Genome Bisulfite Sequencing with Somatic Mutations to Define Epigenomic Drivers
proposals.motivation: Large-scale cancer genomics projects like TCGA have cataloged somatic mutations and exome sequences, but a comprehensive, whole-genome map of DNA methylation across thousands of tumors is lacking. This gap limits our understanding of how epigenetic dysregulation, a hallmark of cancer, interacts with genetic alterations to drive tumorigenesis across diverse cancer types. Profiling the full methylome at scale is now technically feasible and is critical to identify non-coding, epigenomic driver events.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating whole-genome DNA methylation profiles with matched exome sequencing data from thousands of tumors will reveal recurrent, cancer-type-specific epigenomic alterations that are independent of genetic mutations and are functionally linked to transposable element dysregulation and genomic imprinting defects.
proposals.method: We will perform whole-genome bisulfite sequencing (WGBS) on ~3,000 tumor-normal pairs from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) archive, spanning at least 20 cancer types. Data will be processed using established pipelines (e.g., Bismark, MethylKit) to generate base-resolution methylation calls. We will integrate these with existing TCGA exome and clinical data. Analysis will focus on identifying differentially methylated regions (DMRs), particularly in regulatory elements and repetitive sequences, using statistical frameworks like methylSig. We will specifically interrogate the stability of hypermethylated 'blocks' and 'canyons' and correlate these with transposable element silencing and loss-of-imprinting events.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available, pan-cancer atlas of whole-genome DNA methylation, directly complementing genetic data. It will systematically identify candidate epigenomic driver events, reveal the interplay between methylation and mutation in shaping cancer genomes, and provide a resource for developing epigenetic biomarkers. The findings will inform strategies for epigenetic therapies, such as DNMT inhibitors, by defining context-specific vulnerabilities.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have used arrays or targeted sequencing, this proposal scales WGBS to a pan-cancer cohort of unprecedented size, enabling the discovery of methylation alterations beyond CpG islands. The integrated analysis with exome data and focused investigation on transposable elements and imprinting control regions builds directly on Dr. Jones's prior work to move from observation to mechanistic insight.

proposals.title: Genetic mutations as drivers of locus-specific DNA hypermethylation in cancer: Testing the reverse causality hypothesis
proposals.motivation: The classical model in cancer epigenetics posits that epigenetic alterations, like promoter hypermethylation, precede and facilitate genetic damage. However, this causality is largely inferred. Recent work, including from our lab, shows that DNMT1 inhibition leads to chromosome-specific retention of hypermethylation, suggesting some epigenetic marks are exceptionally stable and may have a distinct origin. This project directly tests the alternative hypothesis: that primary genetic mutations can predispose to specific, stable epigenetic changes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific somatic mutations (e.g., in chromatin remodelers, transcription factors, or at fragile sites) create a permissive genomic context that drives the establishment and maintenance of aberrant DNA methylation at discrete loci, thereby inverting the proposed causality of epigenetic facilitation.
proposals.method: We will utilize a panel of isogenic human cancer cell lines engineered to harbor specific, recurrent mutations (e.g., in ARID1A, TET2, or at structural variant breakpoints). Using high-throughput profiling (e.g., Illumina EPIC arrays and whole-genome bisulfite sequencing), we will longitudinally track DNA methylation dynamics in these cells post-mutation introduction and during subsequent passages. We will integrate these data with assays for chromatin accessibility (ATAC-seq) and histone modifications (ChIP-seq) to identify the cis-regulatory elements and trans-acting factors implicated. Analysis will employ established algorithms (e.g., MethylKit for DMR identification, HOMER for motif analysis) to link mutation-induced chromatin states to de novo methylation patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental evidence for mutation-driven epigenetic reprogramming in cancer, challenging the unidirectional model of epigenetics-first causality. It will identify novel mechanistic links between genetic lesions and the epigenome, potentially revealing mutation-specific epigenetic vulnerabilities that could be targeted therapeutically.
proposals.novelty: While most studies correlate genetic and epigenetic changes, this proposal actively engineers mutations to test their sufficiency in *causing* specific hypermethylation, a reverse-engineering approach not commonly applied to this question. It leverages pre-2026 insights on stable epigenetic imprints and transposable element regulation to investigate a non-canonical sequence of oncogenic events.

proposals.title: Context-Dependent Mutagenicity of 5-Methylcytosine: A Pan-Cancer Analysis of Somatic Mutation Rates in Relation to Genomic Features and Epigenetic States
proposals.motivation: 5-methylcytosine (5mC) is a well-known endogenous mutagen due to its spontaneous deamination to thymine, contributing to C-to-T transitions at CpG sites. However, its mutagenic rate is typically treated as uniform, despite evidence that mutation rates vary across the genome. This project will systematically test if 5mC mutagenicity is modulated by local genomic context, chromatin state, and cancer type, which is critical for understanding mutational signatures and cancer evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: The mutagenic potential of 5-methylcytosine is not universal but is significantly influenced by genomic features (e.g., replication timing, chromatin accessibility, transposable element proximity) and varies across cancer types due to differences in underlying epigenetic landscapes.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-coverage whole-genome sequencing data from ICGC/TCGA across multiple cancer types with matched epigenomic profiles (e.g., DNA methylation arrays, histone modification ChIP-seq, ATAC-seq). Using custom computational pipelines, we will calculate context-specific C-to-T transition rates at methylated vs. unmethylated CpG dinucleotides, stratifying by genomic features. Statistical modeling (generalized linear mixed models) will test for interactions between 5mC status, genomic context, and cancer type, controlling for covariates like replication timing and transcriptional activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive map of variable 5mC mutagenicity, identifying genomic 'hotspots' and 'coldspots' for this key mutational process. It will refine models of somatic evolution in cancer and may reveal epigenetic vulnerabilities linked to mutation accumulation, with implications for understanding treatment-related mutagenesis.
proposals.novelty: While 5mC's role as a mutagen is established, its variability has been underexplored. This study moves beyond a uniform model to systematically dissect how the local epigenetic and genomic environment modulates its mutagenic effect across cancers, leveraging Dr. Jones's expertise in methylation dynamics and imprinting.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Epigenetic Second Hit: A Genome-Wide Analysis of DNA Hypermethylation and Genetic Lesions in Tumor Suppressor Gene Inactivation
proposals.motivation: Knudson's two-hit hypothesis is a cornerstone of cancer genetics, but its expansion to include epigenetic silencing via promoter hypermethylation remains poorly quantified. Pre-2026 studies, including Dr. Jones's work on DNMT1 inhibition, show that DNA methylation is a stable, somatically heritable lesion, yet the relative frequency and combinatorial rules of epigenetic versus genetic hits across tumor types are unknown. This gap hinders a complete understanding of tumor suppressor inactivation landscapes and the therapeutic targeting of epigenetic lesions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that promoter hypermethylation serves as a frequent 'second hit' following a genetic first hit (mutation or deletion) in many cancers, and that specific tumor suppressor genes and chromosomal contexts show a bias towards epigenetic versus genetic inactivation mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will integrate whole-genome bisulfite sequencing (WGBS) and whole-genome sequencing (WGS) data from pre-2026 TCGA cohorts (e.g., COAD, BRCA, LUSC) for ~500 matched tumor-normal pairs. Using established pipelines (Bismark for WGBS, Mutect2 for WGS), we will identify biallelic inactivation events for a curated panel of tumor suppressor genes. For each gene, we will classify hits as genetic (mut/deletion) or epigenetic (hyper-methylation) and statistically determine their co-occurrence patterns. Chromosome-specific retention of hypermethylation, as seen in Jones's prior work, will be analyzed using custom scripts to assess genomic imprinting and transposable element contexts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative, genome-wide map of epigenetic hit frequency in the Knudson framework, defining 'epigenetic hit signatures' for specific cancer types. It will clarify the clinical relevance of DNA methylation as a driver event and inform patient stratification for epigenetic therapies like DNMT inhibitors, by identifying tumors where silencing is primarily maintained by methylation.
proposals.novelty: While epigenetic silencing is known, systematically quantifying it as a discrete 'hit' within the canonical Knudson model and defining its combinatorial rules with genetic lesions across primary patient genomes is a novel, data-driven approach to cancer initiation.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Reconstruction of Clonal DNA Methylation Evolution in Colorectal Cancer
proposals.motivation: DNA methylation alterations are key drivers of cancer, but their clonal origin and evolutionary trajectory within tumors remain poorly mapped. Pre-2026 work, including Jones's own studies on DNMT1 inhibition and methylation dynamics, highlights the stability and heterogeneity of cancer-associated hypermethylation. Understanding the phylogenetic history of these epigenetic events is crucial for deciphering tumor progression and identifying early, clonal markers for intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that somatic DNA methylation patterns in a tumor can be used to reconstruct robust phylogenetic trees of cellular lineages, and that the branching order of these trees will reveal early, clonal methylation events that are conserved across metastatic sites and distinct from later, subclonal alterations.
proposals.method: We will apply maximum parsimony and neighbor-joining algorithms to high-density methylation array data (Illumina EPIC) from multi-region sampling of primary colorectal tumors and matched metastases. Methylation states at lineage-informative CpG islands will be treated as discrete, heritable characters. We will validate tree robustness using bootstrapping and compare phylogenetic topologies derived from methylation data to those from somatic SNVs obtained from parallel whole-exome sequencing of the same samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel framework for modeling epigenetic evolution in solid tumors, directly linking methylation phylogenies to tumor architecture and metastatic spread. It will identify clonally fixed epigenetic driver events, offering new targets for early detection and for therapies aimed at the foundational epigenetic state of a cancer.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic methods are standard for genomic mutations, their systematic application to genome-wide methylation patterns to trace tumor evolution is novel. This approach moves beyond correlative methylation profiling to a model of causal, heritable epigenetic change across a tumor's life history.

proposals.title: Targeted Function of Gene Body Methylation: Identifying Specific Reader Proteins and Their Role in Transcriptional Regulation in Cancer Cells
proposals.motivation: Gene body methylation (GbM) is a conserved epigenetic feature, but its function remains enigmatic and is often dismissed as transcriptional noise or a byproduct of silencing transposable elements. In cancer, global hypomethylation co-exists with specific hypermethylation events, yet the role of GbM dynamics is poorly understood. Determining if GbM has a targeted regulatory function, mediated by specific molecular readers, would fundamentally shift our understanding of epigenetic control in both normal and malignant cells.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that gene body methylation is a targeted epigenetic mark recognized by specific reader proteins, which modulate RNA polymerase II processivity or splicing, and that disruption of this system contributes to aberrant gene expression in cancer.
proposals.method: We will perform CRISPR-based knockout screens in isogenic cancer cell lines to identify proteins whose loss specifically alters the transcriptional output of genes with high GbM, but not promoter-methylated genes. Candidate readers (e.g., methyl-CpG-binding domain proteins, chromatin remodelers) will be validated using quantitative ChIP-seq for their enrichment at methylated gene bodies versus promoters. We will integrate these data with high-throughput bisulfite sequencing (WGBS) and nascent RNA sequencing (PRO-seq) from the same cells, treated with or without DNMT1 inhibitors, to directly link reader binding, GbM dynamics, and transcriptional elongation changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field beyond the 'noise' model by providing the first direct evidence for targeted readers of GbM. It will establish a mechanistic link between a specific epigenetic mark, its interpreters, and a defined transcriptional outcome. Furthermore, it will delineate how pharmacological DNMT inhibition, a cancer treatment strategy, exerts its effects not only via promoter demethylation but also through disrupting GbM-reader complexes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has correlated GbM with gene expression, a direct test for targeted readers and their functional consequence is lacking. Our approach uniquely combines genetic screens, epigenomic profiling, and elongation-specific assays to move from correlation to causation, leveraging Dr. Jones's expertise in DNMT inhibition and methylation dynamics.

proposals.title: Inferring Causal Regulatory Networks Linking DNMT1-Inhibitor-Induced DNA Methylation Dynamics to Transcriptional Outcomes in Cancer Cells
proposals.motivation: Pharmacological inhibition of DNMT1 (e.g., with 5-azacytidine) induces genome-wide DNA hypomethylation, but specific loci, particularly in cancer, show persistent hypermethylation, as shown in Jones's prior work. The causal relationships between these targeted methylation changes and subsequent gene expression alterations remain poorly mapped, limiting our understanding of the drug's therapeutic and resistance mechanisms. A systematic, computational inference of these relationships is needed to move beyond correlation and identify key regulatory drivers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that network inference applied to matched time-course methylation (e.g., RRBS, arrays) and RNA-seq data from DNMT1-inhibitor-treated cancer cells will reveal a core set of causal, directed relationships where methylation changes at specific regulatory elements (e.g., enhancers, promoters of imprinted genes/transposable elements) are predictive of expression changes in downstream target genes.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and newly generated time-series multi-omics data from cancer cell lines treated with DNMT1 inhibitors. Using the Causal Inference Test (CIT) and algorithms like GENIE3 or context-specific Likelihood Equivalence (LE) based methods, we will construct directed gene-regulatory networks. Inputs will include methylation beta-values at CpG islands, shores, and enhancer regions (annotated via ENCODE/H3K27ac ChIP-seq) and paired expression data. The analysis will be stratified by genomic context, with a focus on imprinted loci and transposable element regions, given their known methylation sensitivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first causal network model linking DNMT1 inhibition dynamics to transcriptional regulation, identifying key methylation 'driver' events. It will functionally validate the biological significance of retained hypermethylation sites observed in prior studies. The resulting network will be a resource for predicting combination therapies and understanding epigenetic therapy resistance.
proposals.novelty: While correlations between methylation and expression are well-studied, applying rigorous causal network inference to pharmacologically induced, time-resolved epigenetic dynamics in cancer is novel. This approach uniquely integrates Jones's expertise on locus-specific methylation retention with computational causality to move from descriptive profiling to mechanistic modeling.

proposals.title: Genome-wide mapping of active DNA demethylation dynamics in cancer cells following DNMT1 inhibition
proposals.motivation: While Dr. Jones's prior work has characterized the retention of DNA hypermethylation after DNMT1 inhibition, the reciprocal process of active DNA demethylation remains poorly mapped and understood in a cancer context. Pre-2026 knowledge indicates that active demethylation, mediated by TET enzymes and base excision repair, is crucial for gene regulation, imprinting, and erasing aberrant methylation, but its genome-wide targets and functional consequences in patient-derived cancer cells are unclear. This gap limits our understanding of the full epigenetic response to demethylating therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pharmacological inhibition of DNMT1 in cancer cells triggers targeted, locus-specific active DNA demethylation at key regulatory elements, and that these sites are functionally linked to the re-expression of tumor suppressor genes and transposable elements.
proposals.method: We will treat patient-derived colon cancer cell lines and matched normal epithelial cells with the DNMT1 inhibitor GSK-3685032. Active demethylation sites will be identified using a pre-2026 established method: glucosylation of 5-hydroxymethylcytosine (5hmC) followed by enrichment and deep sequencing (hMeDIP-seq or oxidative bisulfite sequencing). We will integrate this with whole-genome bisulfite sequencing (WGBS) to distinguish active loss from passive dilution. Bioinformatic analysis will use established pipelines (e.g., MethylKit, SeSAMe) to map dynamics, correlate with RNA-seq data, and identify sequence and chromatin features using HOMER for motif enrichment.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first comprehensive map of active versus passive DNA demethylation dynamics in response to a clinically relevant DNMT1 inhibitor. It will define the regulatory elements and gene networks most susceptible to active demethylation, linking this process directly to transcriptional outcomes. The work will refine our mechanistic understanding of epigenetic cancer therapy and identify potential biomarkers for response.
proposals.novelty: This proposal directly inverts the focus from retained hypermethylation (Jones's prior work) to the active demethylation response, applying state-of-the-art 5hmC mapping to a therapeutic context. The systematic integration of 5hmC dynamics, WGBS, and transcriptional data in patient-derived models to dissect functional roles is novel and non-obvious.

proposals.title: CRISPR-dCas9-Mediated Targeted DNA Demethylation to Reactivate Imprinted Tumor Suppressor ncRNAs in Colorectal Cancer
proposals.motivation: Aberrant DNA hypermethylation at specific loci, such as imprinting control regions (ICRs), is a hallmark of cancer and can stably silence tumor suppressor genes and noncoding RNAs (e.g., nc886). Pre-2026 pharmacological DNMT inhibitors like decitabine are global and cytotoxic, limiting their therapeutic use. A method for locus-specific, heritable epigenetic reversal is needed to test the causal role of specific methylation events and develop targeted therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeting a CRISPR-dCas9-TET1 catalytic domain fusion to the hypermethylated ICR of the imprinted nc886 locus will induce specific, stable DNA demethylation, leading to sustained re-expression of this putative tumor suppressor RNA and impaired growth in colorectal cancer cells.
proposals.method: We will engineer a lentiviral system expressing dCas9 fused to the catalytic domain of human TET1. sgRNAs will be designed to target the differentially methylated region (DMR) controlling nc886 imprinting, identified from prior mouse and patient methylome data. Colorectal cancer cell lines with hypermethylated, silenced nc886 will be transduced. Demethylation efficiency will be quantified by pyrosequencing and bisulfite cloning. Functional outcomes will be assessed by measuring nc886 expression (RT-qPCR), cell proliferation, and colony formation assays over multiple passages to assess stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a precise tool to dissect the functional consequence of erasing a single, cancer-specific hypermethylated mark, moving beyond association to causation. It will provide proof-of-concept for a novel therapeutic strategy that combines the specificity of CRISPR with the durable epigenetic effects of TET-mediated demethylation, potentially avoiding the genotoxicity and broad effects of global DNMT inhibitors.
proposals.novelty: While dCas9-effector fusions exist, applying them to reverse disease-linked hypermethylation at an imprinted, noncoding RNA locus—integrating Dr. Jones's expertise in methylation, imprinting, and cancer—is novel. The focus on achieving a stable, heritable epigenetic 'edit' rather than transient transcriptional modulation addresses a key limitation in the field.

proposals.title: Comparative Profiling of DNA Demethylating Agents Reveals Agent-Specific Induction of Mesenchymal Phenotypes in Murine Fibroblasts
proposals.motivation: DNA methyltransferase inhibitors (DNMTis) like 5-aza-2'-deoxycytidine (decitabine) are established epigenetic cancer therapies, but their effects on cellular plasticity beyond direct cytotoxicity are not fully understood. Pre-2026 work by Jones and others has shown that DNMT inhibition can induce lineage-specific gene expression and alter imprinting, suggesting a broader role in cellular reprogramming. Testing other demethylating agents is crucial to determine if mesenchymal phenotype induction is a general consequence of DNA hypomethylation or a drug-specific effect, with implications for both therapeutic efficacy and unintended side-effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that structurally distinct DNA demethylating agents will induce mesenchymal phenotypes in 10T1/2 and 3T3 murine fibroblast cells, but that the spectrum and stability of these phenotypes will vary based on the agent's mechanism of action and off-target effects.
proposals.method: We will treat 10T1/2 and 3T3 cells with a panel of DNA demethylating agents, including decitabine, its analog 5-azacytidine, and the non-nucleoside agent RG108. Phenotypic changes will be assessed via phase-contrast microscopy and quantified by high-content imaging for morphological metrics (e.g., cell elongation, area). Mesenchymal marker expression (e.g., Vimentin, N-cadherin) will be validated by immunofluorescence and qRT-PCR. Genome-wide DNA methylation dynamics will be profiled using reduced representation bisulfite sequencing (RRBS) to correlate hypomethylation patterns, particularly at transposable elements and imprinting control regions, with phenotypic outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will systematically map the relationship between specific demethylating chemistries and induced cellular plasticity, providing a resource for selecting agents with desired epigenetic and phenotypic outcomes. It will clarify whether mesenchymal transformation is a universal response to DNA hypomethylation, informing the design of safer epigenetic therapies and models of epigenetic reprogramming.
proposals.novelty: While DNMTis are known to alter differentiation, a direct, comparative screen linking different demethylating agents to specific mesenchymal phenotypes in isogenic cell models has not been performed. This approach moves beyond assessing cytotoxicity or global methylation loss to define agent-specific epigenetic and morphological signatures.

proposals.title: Defining the Locus-Specific DNA Demethylation Landscape Required for Reversion to a Pluripotential State
proposals.motivation: The reversion of differentiated or cancer cells to a pluripotential state, whether through reprogramming or drug treatment, holds immense therapeutic promise. However, the precise DNA methylation changes that are necessary and sufficient for this reversion remain poorly defined at a genome-wide, locus-specific level. Understanding this molecular mechanism is critical for improving the efficiency and safety of regenerative medicine and for developing novel epigenetic cancer therapies that exploit cellular plasticity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reversion to a pluripotential state is driven by the targeted demethylation of a specific, limited set of CpG islands and enhancers associated with core pluripotency transcription factors, rather than by global, non-specific hypomethylation.
proposals.method: We will utilize an inducible cellular reprogramming system (e.g., OSKM induction) and a DNMT1-inhibitor treatment model (e.g., 5-aza-2'-deoxycytidine) to induce reversion. High-throughput DNA methylation profiling will be performed using whole-genome bisulfite sequencing (WGBS) at multiple time points. We will employ a bioinformatics pipeline integrating Bismark for alignment and MethylKit for differential methylation analysis to identify loci where demethylation is temporally correlated with the expression of pluripotency markers. Candidate loci will be functionally validated using CRISPR-dCas9-TET1 targeted demethylation followed by assays for pluripotency gene activation and cellular phenotype.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a high-resolution map of the essential DNA methylation changes required for pluripotency reversion, distinguishing causal events from bystander effects. It will identify novel epigenetic gatekeepers of the pluripotent state and provide a mechanistic framework for understanding how DNMT inhibitors and reprogramming factors converge on a common epigenetic endpoint.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has cataloged methylation changes during reprogramming, this proposal uniquely focuses on defining the *causal* subset of these changes by integrating pharmacological and genetic reversion models with temporal high-resolution sequencing and targeted functional epigenomic editing, moving beyond correlation to establish mechanism.

proposals.title: Epigenetic Stabilization of Cellular Identity: Using SAM and Folate to Lock a Differentiated Phenotype via Targeted DNA Hypermethylation
proposals.motivation: A major challenge in cell-based therapies and disease modeling is the instability of induced cell states, where differentiated cells can revert or drift. Pre-2026, most epigenetic cancer therapies focus on demethylating agents (e.g., DNMT inhibitors like azacitidine) to reactivate silenced genes. This proposal inverts that paradigm, asking if we can instead use methylation to enforce a stable, therapeutic phenotype, directly addressing the instability seen in induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived lineages and transdifferentiated cells.
proposals.hypothesis: Supplementation with the methyl donor S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) and folate, following the induction of a target cellular phenotype, will induce targeted DNA hypermethylation at key pluripotency and progenitor gene loci, thereby epigenetically 'locking' the cells in the desired differentiated state and preventing regression.
proposals.method: We will use a murine in vitro model of neuronal differentiation from iPSCs. Following successful differentiation induction (confirmed by β-III-tubulin expression), cells will be treated with a combination of SAM and folic acid. Genome-wide DNA methylation dynamics will be profiled using high-throughput reduced representation bisulfite sequencing (RRBS), as previously established in the Jones lab. We will specifically analyze methylation changes at promoter regions of core pluripotency factors (e.g., Oct4, Nanog) and neuronal progenitor genes, correlating these with transcriptional silencing (by RNA-seq) and long-term phenotypic stability over multiple passages.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for a novel 'epigenetic locking' strategy, moving beyond demethylation-focused therapies. It will delineate the specific loci susceptible to stabilization via nutrient-driven hypermethylation and provide a mechanistic link between methyl donor availability, targeted epigenetic modification, and long-term cellular identity. This could inform new approaches for regenerative medicine and controlling cellular plasticity in cancer.
proposals.novelty: While Jones's prior work extensively characterized the dynamics and inhibition of DNA methylation, this proposal actively exploits the methylation machinery to enforce stability, a functional inversion of conventional epigenetic therapy. It uniquely combines differentiation biology with methyl donor pharmacology, a non-obvious approach given the field's predominant focus on erasing methylation marks in cancer.

proposals.title: Spatial Epigenomic Profiling of Intratumoral Heterogeneity via In Situ Methylation Capture and Transcriptomic Co-detection
proposals.motivation: Current cancer epigenomics, particularly DNA methylation analysis, relies on bulk or single-cell sequencing, which destroys the spatial architecture of the tumor microenvironment. This spatial context is critical for understanding how epigenetic dysregulation, such as the locus-specific hypermethylation Dr. Jones has documented, varies across tumor subregions, stromal interactions, and treatment-resistant niches. Integrating this with spatial transcriptomics would reveal how epigenetic states directly influence local gene expression programs in situ.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting spatial transcriptomics platforms (e.g., 10x Genomics Visium) for simultaneous capture of DNA methylation information and polyadenylated RNA from the same tissue section will reveal spatially resolved, functionally linked epigenomic-transcriptomic states that drive intratumoral heterogeneity and therapy response.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 10x Visium platform by designing spatially barcoded capture probes complementary to bisulfite-converted DNA at key regulatory elements (e.g., promoters, enhancers, transposable elements) implicated in Jones' prior work, alongside standard mRNA-capture probes. Tissue sections from patient-derived xenograft models of cancers with known methylation drivers will be processed for on-slide bisulfite conversion, followed by simultaneous capture and library construction. Data analysis will involve spatial mapping of methylation states using algorithms like Seurat for spatial clustering and novel computational pipelines to correlate local methylation density (e.g., at imprinted loci like nc886) with adjacent transcriptional output.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first robust method for spatially resolved, multi-omic profiling of DNA methylation and transcription from intact tissue. It will directly link regional epigenetic dysregulation to transcriptional programs within the tumor microenvironment, providing a new lens to identify spatially constrained biomarkers of disease progression and treatment resistance, such as pockets of cells with retained hypermethylation after DNMT inhibitor treatment.
proposals.novelty: While spatial transcriptomics is established, its adaptation for targeted epigenomic capture, specifically for DNA methylation, is unexplored. The novelty lies in the co-profiling chemistry and the computational integration framework to map epigenetic heterogeneity in its native tissue architecture, moving beyond dissociative single-cell or bulk analyses.

proposals.title: Epigenomic Profiling of Normal Colonic Epithelium in High-Risk Patients Reveals DNA Methylation Signatures Associated with Resistance to Neoplastic Transformation
proposals.motivation: While extensive research has characterized the epigenomic landscape of cancer, the epigenetic state of normal cells that resist transformation in high-risk individuals remains poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to identify protective mechanisms and develop strategies to reinforce them. Studying these 'resistant' cells in patients with conditions like ulcerative colitis or Lynch syndrome could reveal novel, therapeutically actionable epigenetic barriers to carcinogenesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of histologically normal epithelial cells in tissues from high-risk patients harbor a distinct, stable DNA methylation profile—characterized by hypermethylation of specific transposable elements and/or hypomethylation of key developmental enhancers—that confers resistance to oncogenic transformation.
proposals.method: We will perform high-depth whole-genome bisulfite sequencing (WGBS) and RNA-seq on laser-capture microdissected, histologically normal colonic crypts from high-risk patients (e.g., with longstanding ulcerative colitis) and matched healthy controls. Using an unsupervised clustering approach (e.g., non-negative matrix factorization) on the methylation data, we will identify epigenomic subtypes within the 'normal' tissue. We will then apply a random forest classifier, trained on these subtypes and validated in an independent cohort, to pinpoint specific differentially methylated regions (DMRs) and transposable element families (e.g., LINE-1, Alu) whose methylation status is most predictive of a 'resistant' epigenotype. Functional validation will involve targeted demethylation of candidate protective DMRs in primary human colonic organoids followed by exposure to genotoxic stress.
proposals.contribution: This work will define, for the first time, a protective epigenomic signature in normal human tissue at risk for cancer. It will move the field beyond cataloging drivers of transformation to identifying endogenous suppressors of it. The findings could lead to biomarkers for refined risk stratification and novel epigenetic strategies for cancer prevention.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the focus from transformed cells to resistant normal cells within the same high-risk microenvironment, and in applying high-resolution methylation profiling to deconvolve this heterogeneity. This contrasts with prior studies that typically compare bulk normal tissue from healthy donors to cancerous tissue.

ideas.description: This approach develops a platform for the concurrent mapping of DNA methylation, histone modifications, and genetic sequence from the same native chromatin molecule without consuming it. It employs a sequential, multi-modal detection strategy within a single device, beginning with non-destructive optical or electrical sensing of epigenetic marks followed by direct long-read sequencing of the same molecule. This ensures all epigenetic layers are directly aligned to the precise genetic sequence and haplotype from which they originated, eliminating inference and enabling true multi-omics correlation at single-molecule resolution.

ideas.title: Context-Filtered Identification of Functional miRNA Targets Using Integrated Functional Genomics

Crucially, it then filters these predictions through multiple layers of experimental evidence from the same cellular context, including AGO2 binding sites (from CLIP-seq) and transcripts showing decreased ribosome occupancy (from polysome profiling). Only targets that are both computationally predicted and show evidence of functional engagement in the specific epigenetic state are prioritized for experimental validation, ensuring the identified interactions are biologically consequential.

ideas.title: A Standardized Environmental Stress Panel to Profile Epigenetic Drug Effects on miRNAs Across Cancers
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecotoxicology's use of standardized model organism panels to assess chemical effects across ecosystems, this research adapts the solution to cancer epigenetics. The idea is to develop a standardized environmental stress panel for cancer cells, comprising a curated set of cell lines from diverse cancer types and a defined panel of epigenetic drugs. By applying this panel uniformly, the research will systematically profile miRNA expression changes, enabling the distinction between conserved, drug-class-specific 'core' miRNA regulatory responses and cancer-lineage-specific 'contextual' responses, thereby creating a comparative map of epigenetic drug effects across the cancer landscape.

ideas.title: Causal Integration of Epigenomic Aberrations and Genomic Variants (ICE-GV)
ideas.description: This system tackles the inverse problem by moving from direct inference to causal integration. It begins with high-resolution epigenomic profiling to identify aberrant regulatory states, then uses these as priors to guide the analysis of matched whole-genome sequencing data for mutations in chromatin-associated genes. A probabilistic causal model evaluates the likelihood that a specific mutation explains the observed epigenomic pattern by incorporating functional genomic annotations, co-segregation analysis in pedigrees or cell lineages, and targeted functional validation. The output is not a simple prediction but a ranked set of candidate causal mutations with associated confidence scores based on convergent evidence.

ideas.title: Epigenetic Primacy: Testing if Epigenetic Dysfunction Can Initiate Oncogenic Trajectories Independent of Driver Mutations
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that epigenetic alterations in cancer are always secondary to genetic mutations by drawing an analogy from neurodegenerative disease. In neuroscience, the solution to understanding disease spread was the 'prion-like propagation' hypothesis, where a misfolded protein state acts as a primary, self-templating driver. Adapted to cancer, this proposes that specific, heritable epigenetic dysregulations could themselves be primary oncogenic drivers. The research would test if transmissible, self-propagating epigenetic states can initiate oncogenic trajectories in genetically normal cells, positioning epigenetic dysfunction not as a consequence, but as a potential cause of malignant transformation.

ideas.title: Apply ChIP-seq with temporal stratification to map epigenetic dynamics at the miR-127 locus
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecology, where population responses to environmental disturbances are mapped through stratified temporal sampling, this idea adapts that solution to epigenetic mapping. The analogous ecological solution involves collecting samples at multiple defined intervals after a disturbance to model dynamic trajectories, rather than taking single 'before and after' snapshots. The adapted research idea applies this temporal stratification to ChIP-seq analysis. After drug treatment, chromatin is immunoprecipitated for specific histone modifications at the miR-127 locus not just once, but at a series of sequential time points (e.g., 30 minutes, 2, 8, 24, and 72 hours post-treatment). This approach will generate a kinetic profile of epigenetic changes, revealing whether certain histone marks are early responders or late effects, how long modifications persist, and the potential sequence of events leading to the stable epigenetic state observed in traditional single-time-point experiments.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: From silencing pattern to sequence

ideas.title: PROTAC-Informed Degrader Design for Challenging Epigenetic Targets
ideas.description: This approach addresses the difficulty of designing high-affinity inhibitors for non-catalytic epigenetic targets by shifting the strategy from occupancy-based inhibition to event-driven degradation. It employs fragment-based screening to identify weak but specific binders to the target's challenging surface, then rationally links these to ligands for a suitable E3 ubiquitin ligase to create a PROTAC. The resulting degrader molecule catalytically eliminates the target protein, providing a more robust and specific probe to study its biological function, with built-in validation through observable protein loss and consequent phenotypic changes in cellular models.

ideas.title: A Causal Network Inference Framework for Epigenetic-Mutation Interactions in Cancer
ideas.description: This research adapts the causal attribution methodology from climate science to systematically link epigenetic abnormalities to genetic mutations in cancer. The analogous problem in climate science is attributing extreme weather events to specific human activities, solved using probabilistic event attribution with model ensembles comparing worlds with and without anthropogenic forcings. The adapted idea proposes a computational framework that, for a given cancer cohort, creates in silico counterfactual models—one with the observed mutational and epigenomic landscape and another where a candidate driver mutation is computationally 'removed' or its immediate effects reversed. By analyzing large ensembles of these paired models across many tumors and using statistical comparison of downstream molecular phenotypes (e.g., transcription factor binding, chromatin states, pathway activation), the research aims to move beyond correlation to probabilistically attribute specific epigenetic dysregulation events (like hypermethylation or aberrant enhancer activation) to the causal influence of particular genetic mutations, clarifying their interplay in cancer initiation and progression.

It combines targeted epigenetic editing tools for locus-specific manipulation with staggered pharmacological perturbations to control the order of pathway engagement. A factorial experimental design and interaction modeling framework are used to statistically distinguish between independent, synergistic, or antagonistic effects. This approach moves beyond simple decoupling to build a dynamic, mechanistic model of how these two epigenetic pathways intersect to regulate miRNA expression.

ideas.title: Epigenetic Reversal as an Ecological Intervention: Assessing System-Wide Consequences
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that reversing cancer-specific epigenetic abnormalities always benefits patients by drawing an analogy from ecosystem restoration ecology. In that field, the aggressive removal of an invasive species—seen as the clear problem—can paradoxically cause system collapse ('restoration shock') because the ecosystem has adapted to its presence. The analogous solution involves careful, phased intervention and modeling of cascading effects. Adapted to cancer epigenetics, this suggests researching the potential adverse effects of epigenetic therapies by viewing the cancer cell as a dysregulated but stable state where specific epigenetic changes are embedded within a network of compensatory cellular processes. The research would aim to identify which epigenetic reversals might destabilize the cell in ways that lead to lack of efficacy (e.g., by activating bypass pathways) or direct harm (e.g., triggering extreme stress responses or unmasking latent vulnerabilities without a therapeutic window), thereby refining therapeutic strategies to avoid such 'therapeutic shock'.

ideas.title: Mechanistically-Grounded Epigenetic Network Analysis via Constrained Ecological Analogy
ideas.description: This approach begins with a formal ontological alignment step to identify precise, biophysically justified analogies between ecological interactions (e.g., predation, mutualism) and epigenetic mechanisms (e.g., competitive histone binding, cooperative chromatin looping). Using this constrained mapping, it constructs epigenetic interaction networks where edge types and weights are defined by mechanistic relationships rather than mere correlation. The resulting models are then stress-tested through targeted experimental perturbations to validate their predictive power for epigenetic state changes, ensuring the network analysis yields causal insight into regulatory dynamics.

ideas.title: Adapting Network Theory from Ecology to Map the miRNA-Target Interactome
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the analogous problem of reconstructing incomplete ecological networks (e.g., food webs) to the challenge of identifying additional mRNA targets for a set of epigenetically activated miRNAs. In ecology, known species interactions are combined with environmental and trait data in a Bayesian framework to predict missing links, which are then field-validated. Adapted to molecular biology, the solution involves constructing a prior network from the 17 miRNAs and their known/validated targets, then using a Bayesian integrative model that incorporates sequence complementarity (from target prediction algorithms), inversely correlated expression patterns (from transcriptomic data), and the shared epigenetic context (e.g., common upstream histone marks) to score and rank potential novel mRNA targets. The highest-probability 'missing links' in this regulatory network are prioritized for experimental validation using techniques such as CLIP-seq to confirm direct binding or multiplexed luciferase assays for functional repression, thereby systematically expanding the mapped miRNA-target interactome.

ideas.title: Adapting Antiviral 'Shock and Kill' to Silence Oncogenic miRNAs
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'shock and kill' strategy from HIV latency research to challenge the assumption that chromatin-modifying drugs only activate genes. In HIV cure research, latency-reversing agents 'shock' dormant virus into activity so it can be 'killed.' Analogously, we propose to test if a chromatin-modifying drug (the 'shock') can be used to first alter the repressed/heterochromatic state of an oncogenic miRNA locus, making it more amenable to targeting. This epigenetic perturbation would then be immediately followed by a 'kill' agent—a sequence-specific oligonucleotide or CRISPR-based repressor—to bind the accessible locus and establish durable transcriptional repression, thereby inactivating the oncogenic miRNA.

ideas.title: Deciphering the Epigenetic-Metabolic Feedback Circuitry in Cancer Using Ecological Succession Models
ideas.description: This research idea investigates the interconnection between metabolic changes and epigenetic abnormalities in cancer by drawing an analogy from systems ecology, specifically the concept of ecological succession. The analogous solution involves modeling how pioneer species alter an environment to facilitate a new stable state. Adapted to cancer biology, the idea is to treat oncogenic metabolic shifts as pioneer events that remodel the intracellular metabolite landscape (e.g., α-KG, SAM, NAD+ levels), thereby directing epigenetic reprogramming. This epigenetic state then feedbacks to entrench the metabolic phenotype. The project would employ computational systems biology to map these dynamic feedback circuits and experimentally identify critical leverage points where disrupting the metabolic-epigenetic dialogue could reverse the cancer 'successional state' to a more normal cellular phenotype.

ideas.title: Inverse Problem: From Epigenome to Causative Mutation via Seismic Tomography-Inspired Inference
ideas.description: This research adapts the geophysical inverse problem solution of seismic tomography to infer causative genetic mutations from observed epigenetic aberration patterns. In seismology, surface wave data is inverted to model the Earth's hidden interior structure. Here, the multi-dimensional epigenetic state (the 'surface') is the observable output. We propose to build a biophysical forward model that maps from a space of possible genetic variants (e.g., non-coding SNPs, structural variants) to expected epigenetic aberration patterns, incorporating known mechanisms of transcription factor binding disruption, chromatin looping alteration, and spreading of silencing marks. By applying inversion algorithms (e.g., Bayesian inference, gradient descent) to this model using population-scale epigenomic data as input, we can iteratively solve for the most likely 'hidden' genetic architecture responsible for the observed epigenetic dysregulation, scaling the inference from local loci to genome-wide scans.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Signal Denoising for Robust Epigenetic Marker Discovery
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where machine learning is used to identify critical, predictive signals within noisy seismic data for event forecasting. The analogous problem in epigenetics is discovering robust biomarkers from high-dimensional, noisy datasets containing technical and biological variation. By employing deep learning architectures similar to those used for seismic denoising and pattern isolation, we can process large-scale epigenetic data (e.g., methylation arrays, histone modification maps) to filter out non-specific variation and pinpoint sparse, consistent epigenetic signatures that serve as reliable markers for disease classification, prognosis, or treatment response.

It trains models on multiple, diverse epigenetic datasets while explicitly penalizing associations driven by technical or demographic confounders, forcing the discovery of invariant biological signals. The pipeline further prioritizes markers supported by evidence from experimental perturbations or natural longitudinal variation to infer causal relevance. This results in epigenetic signatures that are not only statistically predictive but also biologically interpretable and robust across populations and measurement platforms.

ideas.title: Epigenomic Flight Recorders: Continuous, Low-Burden Monitoring via Epigenetic Wearables
ideas.description: This idea adapts the aerospace concept of continuous flight data recording to longitudinal epigenomic monitoring. In aerospace, black boxes log system parameters continuously for later forensic analysis. The adapted solution is to create a clinical 'epigenomic flight recorder'—a wearable or implantable device that performs continuous, low-burden biosampling (e.g., microneedle array capturing interstitial fluid). This device would stabilize cell-free DNA or other epigenetic material over an extended period (e.g., one month). The patient wears it during treatment, and the stored sample cartridge is retrieved at a clinic visit. The epigenomic data from the continuous log is then analyzed to reconstruct a detailed timeline of treatment response, pinpointing the earliest molecular signatures of resistance or relapse long before clinical symptoms appear, thereby scaling monitoring across time with minimal patient disruption.

ideas.title: Synthetic Lethal Targeting of Validated Writer-Reader Node Pairs via Epistasis Mapping
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea moves beyond a generic dual-targeting strategy by first systematically mapping the functional redundancy and compensatory relationships between chromatin writers and readers. Using high-content genetic screening in disease-relevant cellular contexts, it identifies specific, non-redundant writer-reader pairs whose combined inhibition is synthetically lethal. The approach then focuses drug discovery efforts on developing precision degraders or bivalent inhibitors against this validated, high-confidence node pair, thereby maximizing therapeutic efficacy while minimizing toxicity from broad pathway disruption.

ideas.title: Test miR-127 tumor suppressor role using ecological predator-prey population dynamics model
ideas.description: This idea adapts the problem-solving approach from theoretical ecology, where the functional role of a top predator in suppressing prey is tested. The analogous solution uses mathematical models and controlled experiments to quantify impact on population parameters. Adapted to the miR-127 context, we propose treating the microRNA as a 'predator' and cancer cells as 'prey'. The research would involve engineering inducible miR-127 overexpression in cancer cell lines and then applying ecological population dynamics modeling. We would quantitatively measure miR-127's effect on the cancer cell population's intrinsic growth rate, carrying capacity in 2D culture, and rate of spatial spread/invasion in 3D organoid or collagen matrices. This approach moves beyond simple endpoint assays to provide a dynamic, parameter-rich assessment of miR-127's tumor-suppressive function, identifying whether it primarily affects proliferation, survival saturation density, or invasive dispersal—key insights for potential therapeutic development.

ideas.title: Dynamic Epigenomic Trajectory Modeling for Personalized Prognosis
ideas.description: This research direction integrates high-frequency, longitudinal epigenomic profiling of patients with advanced machine learning, specifically temporal and recurrent neural network architectures, to model disease as a dynamic, personalized process. By analyzing the sequential patterns of epigenetic change over time, the models can identify critical inflection points predictive of treatment resistance or relapse before clinical symptoms manifest. This enables the shift from reactive monitoring to proactive, forecast-driven clinical management, where interventions can be tailored and timed based on predicted trajectory rather than observed outcome. Ultimately, this synergy aims to generate actionable, individual-level prognostic models that continuously update with new patient data, transforming episodic care into adaptive therapy.

ideas.title: Apply single-cell soil microbiome profiling to map tumor microenvironment metabolic niches
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from soil microbial ecology, where single-cell stable isotope probing and Raman microspectroscopy are used to dissect the functional metabolic roles of individual microbes within a complex, heterogeneous community. By transferring this approach to oncology, we propose using analogous single-cell metabolic profiling techniques (e.g., metabolomics, Raman spectroscopy, or imaging mass cytometry for metabolic enzymes) on dissociated tumor samples. The adapted research idea aims to map the metabolic heterogeneity and nutrient exchange networks between cancer cell subpopulations and stromal cells within the tumor microenvironment, thereby identifying critical metabolic niches that drive tumor progression, metastasis, or therapy resistance.
